speaker: Rob Moore
words: Billy?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: First off, I wanted to ask you about when you came west.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did you feel about coming west?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I felt that we may possibly be better in our condition.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think you did better your condition?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, yes, I think we did a little.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did you come out here?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, the Union Pacific.

speaker: William Kauder
words: From Omaha, Nebraska.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it a regular train or a special train?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know, but it was what they called an emigrant train. Gosh, I don't know how many cars there was, but it took two engines to pull us over the rocket.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The train was full of immigrants?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, cars were full.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was that like?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, pretty hard to explain. It was something new to me. I'd never traveled before. I was working on a little farm back there in Missouri.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Another kind of broke up there and went into Iowa and we finished breaking up. That was a poor loaf up in Iowa.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember how much the immigrant train cost?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No. Our tickets were thirty -three dollars apiece.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think it was mostly rich people or poor people who were riding on the immigrant train?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, they looked really like Russians to me. I suppose they were mostly scuttles to the country. They had no solid holdings.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where did you sleep on the train?

speaker: William Kauder
words: We had bumps. Fungalow and Vegas.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I didn't sleep on any of them. They might try to do it. Couldn't do it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How about your household goods? How did you get them out? What? I'd say, how about your household goods? Did they go on the train with you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: We sold everything on our show, in bedclothes. We boxed up the bedclothes. There's two of the fellows who went with us and we put in our baggage for their tickets.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did many people know each other on that train?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I don't know about that. I didn't get acquainted with anyone.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I suppose it was generally occupied by the father and brothers. The one car we never got out of. It stayed right in that one car.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there much socializing on the train?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I didn't hear of any.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, then how did people keep themselves entertained that long trip?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, I don't know. They were pretty quiet in the car we were in. There wasn't no fussing about it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did you do to keep yourself from getting bored all those long days? On the train, you mean?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes. Oh, there was always something new appearing. That is, scenery. We watched the country pretty well.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I remember one place that was in Wyoming. I think we were in a canyon and looked ahead. I don't know how far, of course, but it looked to me like we were going to go into a city and a town of some kind. Then when we got up in there, there was nothing but a lot of piled up rock that looked like buildings.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was somewhere in Montana?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, in Wyoming, in a canyon.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did that trip take?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, we left on the seventeenth day of March and got into Walla Walla just... I believe it was the twenty -first.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What year was that? What year?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Eighteen... And if I remember, I believe it was 1880. Hm.

speaker: William Kauder
words: That's quite a good ways back.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How old were you then?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Twenty -one.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, after you got out here, you worked around the Dayton country for a while, didn't you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah. I worked out for Walla Walla a little bit, but they were not working Walla Walla. They had their crops there and the old farmers was always passing the streets.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The farmers were always what? What were the farmers doing?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Passing around on the streets, walking back. Yeah.

speaker: William Kauder
words: How'd their work done?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So what did you do? Hm? So what did you do?

speaker: William Kauder
words: In Walla Walla? Didn't do anything. Couldn't get a job there. We landed there on the seventeenth of March and had their crops here. Hm. Was nothing doing anymore. I went up to Dayton and got tangled up in a blame's old sawmill, a patooshah. Well, I was busy enough to buy a key by the harness and moved up to the Potlatch.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When you moved up to the Potlatch, were there people there who'd been there a long time when you arrived?

speaker: William Kauder
words: At our destination, you mean.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, when you moved up on Potlatch Ridge, were there already other people there who'd been there quite a while?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Because it had been settled ten years before we come.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was proving up on your homestead hard work?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I guess so. When we moved in there, 10th of March the 6th, you had to cut brush for me to turn the wagon around.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think it was harder on you than most other people up there? Hard what? Do you think it was harder on you than most other people up there?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, it was. Because we had to take what was left.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And the others had a little more of a choice.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you tempted to give it up?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I was at times, yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Like what times?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, it would happen at different times of the year. Sometimes in the winter, first winter was there, I had snow about 3 feet deep over, piled around in that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did most homesteaders have to work out?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kinds of things would they do?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I was fortunate by far more. Some work in the spring, and some in the harvest.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was clearing land usually a cooperative effort?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Not much.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: If you cleared by yourself, how did you manage to move around those big logs and pile them up and things?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Shut down a big tree, cut them into logs, what we could handle, pile them up next to the stump and burn them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you just move them around with horse teams or what? How was that? Did you just move them around with horse teams or what?

speaker: William Kauder
words: The stumps, I mean, and the logs.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Did you just move around with it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah, we had a team,

speaker: William Kauder
words: and we had to clear labs and raise some feet for that team.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When you burned the stumps and the logs and things, did those fires ever get away?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Not from us, never.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they get away on different people?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, not very much. One family about a mile and a half north east from us, they had two grown boys, and they cut off about 20 acres, I guess, slash stick. And then that project called the burn slashing in the fall of the year. My, that was a big fire.

speaker: William Kauder
words: It never got away.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you get much help in proving up from your neighbors?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Very help back and forth.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Are you busy in here?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I'd like to say thank you.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: If you had the chance to homestead again, would you do it over again?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I doubt it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why not?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, in the first place, I could have took the team and the plow and rinses of the ground and did a whole lot better than to try to clear off a patch of wood.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And try to farm it. Of course, we were about 15 or 18 miles from market and all the way downhill to town and all the way uphill coming home. It was a killing job.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Often I got up at 3 o 'clock in the morning a little before, go out and feed the team, cram and harness them, take care of the rest of the stock.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And it's up to the wagon. I get about five miles from home before it's got daylight. And then it'd be about 9 o 'clock at night when I got home. And I'd never sleep for about two hours. We'd get into town about 11 o 'clock, hit the team and climb the wagon, feed them, go do what's shopping you wanted to do. I was able to do.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And just things ready, it'd be about 8, 9 at 7 o 'clock at night when you get home.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What other kinds of things could people do to get ahead other than homesteading?

speaker: William Kauder
words: There wasn't anything much to do. Nobody was able to hire anyone.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How were homestead claims investigated?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How is that? How would you prove up a homestead? How would you go about showing somebody that you had fulfilled your obligation?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, when you proved up, you took two of your numbers.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But do you when you moved on the farm and at the time that you go to prove up? Where did you take them?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Lawson.

speaker: William Kauder
words: The hot cotton.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Not that kind. It still is.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think many people would cheat to some extent on their homestead proving up?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, there were most that put me on this. I don't know. It's generally hard to work the system. You know, they'd post statements like this.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What office would you go to in Lewiston? What office would you go to in Lewiston to prove up?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where in Lewiston would you go?

speaker: William Kauder
words: The US Land Office.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where was that office?

speaker: William Kauder
words: It was located on Main Street and I don't remember the number. Their office was upstairs.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think some people took advantage of the preemption and the stone and timber to get more land than they may have deserved?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I don't know. I never noticed it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did you give up your homestead and work your father's?

speaker: William Kauder
words: There was just the three of us. Father, mother and myself. And when father passed away, just a year or two after he had proved up, I didn't take care of mother. I couldn't live on the whole city and live there and take care of her. So I just dropped it. There was nothing but a... Never was improved. I don't know who's got it now.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your neighbors, the Wells, manage to prove up? Did what? Did the Wells manage to prove up, your neighbors, the Wells?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Wasn't there a family named Wells that moved here with you from Dayton?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they prove up?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah, they proved up. There was three of them.

speaker: William Kauder
words: The big expenses, the three of them, proved up the same kind. There was father, Wells and a man named Gilman.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they all vouch for each other?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yeah.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Wells and Gilman was the dads' right businesses. Dad and Wells was the... Gilman's business.

speaker: William Kauder
words: And so on.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did the Wells become homesteaders?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I guess they had no other way of getting a home.

speaker: William Kauder
words: There's quite a family of them. The old folks, two boys, and I don't know, four or five girls. Three boys. Cotton, George and Aaron.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you prove up your homestead?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you sell a preemption then or what?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I sold it. I got into trouble over it. I didn't. I come out loser on it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did you get into trouble over it?

speaker: William Kauder
words: There were two others. There was a man I sold through. He was really in possession. The other man kind of stuck in and made trouble. When I relived this, I relived this to the first party. The second party bought it in there. They had a lawsuit in Lewiston, the fellow lost.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So you didn't get anything at all for all those years of work you put in there?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I come out loser on it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did that happen very often, do you think?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't I?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you know why they opened up the Indian Reservation for homestead? The what reservation?

speaker: William Kauder
words: The Indian Reservation, the Nez Perce Reservation.

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, no, I don't know exactly.

speaker: William Kauder
words: But the people were crowding the edges of the reservation pretty close.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think it was a good idea to open up the reservation for homestead?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Maybe I spit chair a lot, but it's first. I don't know what was left by the homesteader could have.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the Indians mind this? Did they react badly?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, they were pretty patient fellows. They didn't realize. They didn't want many of them to find their problems. They really didn't know what was left.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think it was a bad deal for them?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No, I don't think so. It didn't turn out any too good. Look how the loss of much quarters, you know, did great.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did most people think about the Indians?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, they kind of respected the Indians. One says to the world, and they dealt with the Indians, rented their farms,

speaker: William Kauder
words: and the Indians was right there to show their share.

speaker: William Kauder
words: You had to make your rentals through the agency at Lapua.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did opening up the reservation help the economy locally?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I think so.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I was there on the pot last, the first one. I guess it was pretty hard, but the more you spent the most, the more you got the taxes and wasn't able to redeem them. And then I took a home and said, over on the reservation.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Back in those days, how much cash money did you need to get by?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, that difference.

speaker: William Kauder
words: If you had been raised a good garden to help yourself,

speaker: William Kauder
words: why, a hundred dollars would take you through the winter.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long would it take you to make that hundred dollars?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, one fall, I worked for a man by the name of Baker, who lived on the edge of the prairie, here on the thrash machine. I put in here a hundred dollars with him. I had my teeth on the dairy cart. That helped us like everything.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were some of the prices of things around 1895, around those times and there? Like how much would a pair of overalls cost you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, I pushed the wheel in 18...

speaker: William Kauder
words: One must take the second side. That is 82.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I'm really not too sure. It might have been 92 right before that depression.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Ninety -two, that's what I mean. 1892. Because 1890, he was elected in 1892.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were the prices of things around that time?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, in 1896,

speaker: William Kauder
words: no.

speaker: William Kauder
words: 1892 or three, three or four. I bought eight pounds of coffee for a dollar. It was in pound packages and it was parched round, but not round.

speaker: William Kauder
words: It was eight pounds for a dollar, if you didn't get the dollar.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was barter common?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What? Barter? Trading?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did people trade instead of using money?

speaker: William Kauder
words: They traded either. I was out of the donwills.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, did you have to pay cash for all your food and supplies or could you pay in wheat or something like that?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Well, we didn't... Father took up a homestead and it was all timber. And the British race of horses would just raise enough feed for the horses and the cow and a couple of three hogs.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the homesteaders or settlers gather camas or cous? Like they do what? Gather camas like the Indians did?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't think I caught them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the homesteaders gather camas?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Camas is a plant that the Indians used to eat.

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't think so. I don't think so. Never heard of any of them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they do quite a bit of hunting?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, some of them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think the land was more productive in those days?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Yes, I do.

speaker: William Kauder
words: You told me one time about play parties and I never quite understood what a play party was.

speaker: William Kauder
words: A play party?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, a lot of young people get together, you know, at a certain house and then play games.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Like a little boy and...

speaker: William Kauder
words: How was that?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Slapped down the way. A boy and girl had a hand and another one would slap her and another one would go around to catch one another. Snap and catch it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was Miller Boy?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I forget those games. It was a long time. We played at Miller. A lot of couples went around in a circle and there was a lot of cheating to be done in that.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Like...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Let's see, how was that a few?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Miller Boy.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh, that's good.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh... Anyhow, you either turned back or you went forward.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Happy is the Miller Boy that stands by the meal, meal turns around and is all prayed with. And they all turned back. The leader stepped forward and the gents fall back. The chances of getting somebody else's girl are losing out altogether.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were those parties very common? I said what? Were those parties very common?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh no, two or three in the winter.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did they bring up kids in those days?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Out on the farm. Give us all a life.

speaker: William Kauder
words: They had a pretty good school too. But there wasn't much land for a couple of gums to quench. They only could have had that for four to six months a year.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think the changes you've seen have been for the better?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh yes, I love them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Which ones do you think haven't been for the better?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh well, the changes was made and the improvement of the properties of the farmers had to get along better.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Had more to eat. I've raised a good garden, a lot of little beggars and turnips. But says it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think people were happier then or now?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't see any happiness in them now.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why do you think you've lived so long?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I don't think so.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I say why do you think you've lived so long?

speaker: William Kauder
words: I have no idea.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Father, he had a bad heart.

speaker: William Kauder
words: He dropped off all us, it wasn't a hard time.

speaker: William Kauder
words: Mother and I had a pretty hard struggle there for a few years.

speaker: William Kauder
words: The first winter you were on the homestead.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You all lived in a little cabin together, didn't you?

speaker: William Kauder
words: We had a one room house and two beds in it. And there was a living room, dining room, kitchen.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you get along pretty well that year?

speaker: William Kauder
words: Oh golly yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't you sometimes feel like you were stepping on each others toes?

speaker: William Kauder
words: No.
